title: How LoRA adapters work
description: Why low-rank update matrices on frozen weights make adaptation cheap and composable.
body: Use when reasoning about why LoRA is cheap and reversible. LoRA freezes the pretrained weights and learns a low-rank product (A·B) added to chosen projection matrices, so only a small fraction of parameters train and the base is untouched. Do not assume rank is free — too low underfits the task, too high erodes the efficiency and forgetting benefits. Actions: choose target modules and rank, train only the adapters, and load or merge them at serving. Validation signals include a parameter count far below the base and a task metric tracking the rank sweep. Risk boundary: adapters are tied to a base; if the base revision is unknown, escalate before loading because misaligned ranks corrupt outputs.
